narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the unique challenges you've likely navigated to get here with your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and LSAT score of 30. Your family comes from a middle-income background, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the path most students take. Unfortunately, the admissions model predicts a 91% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—it's designed to identify students with the strongest likelihood of passing to ensure program success. The biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school grade decile of 1, which is much lower than the typical student's decile of around 5.5, signaling weaker performance relative to peers at that stage. Nearly as influential was your Year 1 decile also at 1, well below the average of about 5.7, suggesting early academic struggles that carried forward. These two stood out as the most powerful drivers compared to other elements in your profile. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, close to the average of 3.21, nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, showing some resilience in your college record. Your middle-income family background pushed a bit toward the failure prediction, as higher incomes often correlate with bar success in the data, while your full-time status and LSAT score had minimal sway. Overall, your early and later school deciles placed you below typical successful students, who tend to rank in the middle or higher deciles. This isn't a judgment on your potential, but a data-driven flag based on patterns from past bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your high school performance through additional coursework or community college classes to improve those deciles, and seeking mentorship programs tailored for Black women in law to build a stronger profile. You're capable of turning this around—many have—and we encourage reapplying after addressing these key areas. Keep pushing; your story matters.
